name: Interstitial / Scheduled-Insertion Intent
summary: Content scheduled into a sequence at positions other than the authored items — a pre-roll before it starts, a mid-roll between items, a post-roll at the end, or an overlay banner during playback. The shared model behind video ad-breaks, an interstitial slide in a deck, and a between-items promo in a carousel: where it inserts, whether it is skippable / forced / dismissible, and whether the sequence resumes where it left off afterward. A member of the advanceable-sequence family (#1179), cross-media (the canonical case is video ad insertion). Owns the scheduling + the dismissal contract; the inserted content itself is the consumer's. Homed in webintents per the ratified distributed placement (#1175).

position.description: Where the content inserts relative to the sequence. pre-roll = before the first item; mid-roll = between two items at a scheduled point; post-roll = after the last item; overlay = on top of the current item without pausing the position (a banner).

dismissal.description: How the viewer can get past it. skippable = a skip becomes available (optionally after a delay); forced = it must complete before the sequence continues; dismissible = an overlay the viewer can close at any time.

resume.description: What happens after the interstitial. resume = return to exactly where the sequence was (mid-roll continues the interrupted item); advance = proceed to the next item.

description:
Inserted, not authored

Sometimes content is scheduled into a sequence at a position the author didn't place in the item list: an ad before a video, a sponsor slide mid-deck, a promo banner over a carousel. The Interstitial intent models that insertion — where it goes, how the viewer gets past it, and whether the sequence resumes afterward.

Cross-media, video-canonical

The canonical case is video ad-breaks (pre/mid/post-roll, skippable countdown, resume-after), and the same contract serves a deck interstitial and a carousel promo — which is why it homes in the advanceable-sequence family (#1179) rather than a deck contract. It owns the scheduling and the dismissal contract; the inserted content is the consumer's concern.

Composition

  • Overlay surface: the overlay position composes Web Portals (out-of-flow surface).
  • Advance kernel: mid-roll insertion pauses the family's advance, then resume/advance decides re-entry.
